title: GOWA Manager Go-vs-Bun Acceptance Thresholds
description: Numeric tolerances that gate the Go backend against the captured Bun baseline. Each threshold expresses the maximum acceptable deviation of the Go measurement from the Bun baseline. A scenario fails if Go exceeds the tolerance. Thresholds are expressed as percentages unless noted otherwise. 'informational' scenarios have no threshold and never cause a failure.
note: Threshold values require owner approval. These are initial proposals based on observed variance in local benchmark runs. Do NOT relax thresholds to make a failure disappear — a failure must be reported as a blocked release with measured regressions.
rationale: Tolerances were derived from observed run-to-run variance in the Bun baseline (3 samples per scenario on a local Windows machine). Scenarios with higher variance (cold startup, WebSocket) received wider tolerances; scenarios with low variance (idle RSS, proxy throughput) received tighter tolerances. The idle RSS threshold is 0% because the Go backend must not consume more memory than Bun at idle — this is a hard requirement, not a tolerance.

metadataMatch.description: Machine metadata must match between Bun and Go baselines. OS, architecture, CPU model, and CPU count must all be identical. A mismatch rejects the comparison entirely.

coldStartup.rationale: Cold startup is dominated by process initialization and first-request JIT/compile overhead, which varies significantly run-to-run (observed ~5% variance even within 5 samples). 50% tolerance accommodates Go's potentially different startup characteristics (compiled binary vs Bun runtime) while still catching catastrophic regressions.

idleRss.rationale: Go must not exceed Bun's idle RSS. This is a hard requirement — the Go backend is expected to be leaner at idle due to being a compiled binary without a JS runtime. 0% tolerance means Go RSS must be <= Bun RSS.

crudLatency.rationale: CRUD operations are SQLite-bound with low variance (observed p95 spread ~10-15%). 30% tolerance allows for Go's database driver differences (modernc.org/sqlite vs Bun's bun:sqlite) while catching meaningful regressions.

proxyHttpThroughput.rationale: HTTP proxy throughput is the core function of GOWA Manager. Observed variance in the Bun baseline was ~7% across 3 samples. 20% tolerance allows for Go's net/http stack differences while ensuring no material throughput regression. 'No regression' means Go throughput must be >= 80% of Bun throughput.

proxyHttpLatency.rationale: Proxy latency p95 showed ~12% variance in the baseline. 25% tolerance accommodates Go's HTTP handling overhead while catching significant latency regressions.

webSocketThroughput.rationale: WebSocket throughput showed ~3% variance in the baseline but WebSocket proxying involves more moving parts (gorilla/coder.websocket vs Bun's native WS). 30% tolerance allows for library-level differences while ensuring stable behavior.

webSocketLatency.rationale: WebSocket per-message latency p95. Same rationale as throughput — library differences justify a wider tolerance.

monitoringCostCpu.rationale: Monitoring CPU cost is inherently noisy (observed 0-0.6% in baseline). 30% tolerance on a near-zero value is generous but the absolute threshold also guards against runaway CPU. If Bun CPU is 0%, Go must also be effectively idle (< 2% absolute).

gracefulShutdown.rationale: Graceful shutdown must be bounded — the process must exit within 5 seconds of SIGTERM/SIGINT. This is an absolute bound, not relative to Bun, because shutdown duration is a correctness/operational requirement. (Note: the Bun baseline showed ~10s which itself may need investigation, but the Go backend must meet the 5s bound regardless.)

executableSize.rationale: Go and Bun produce fundamentally different binary types (Go compiled binary vs Bun standalone bundle). Size differences are expected and not a gating factor.

dockerImageSize.rationale: Docker image size depends on the base image and build approach, which differ between Go and Bun. Informational only.

leakDetection.description: Leak detection thresholds for repeated load runs. Compares beginning vs. end metrics after 1,000 HTTP requests, 500 WebSocket cycles, and 100 process start/stop cycles.

goroutines.rationale: Goroutine count must not grow by more than 20 after all load cycles. Allows for runtime/scheduler goroutine fluctuation while catching true leaks.

rss.rationale: RSS may grow slightly due to runtime caching and GC behavior, but >10% growth after load cycles indicates a leak.

fds.rationale: File descriptor / handle count must not grow by more than 20. Allows for OS-level FD caching while catching descriptor leaks.
